title: Single-Child Families in India: Levels, Trends and Determinants
abstract: AbstractThis paper discusses a relatively new but an emerging demographic phenomenon of single-child families in India. This paper examines the levels, trends and determinants of single-child families using the data from three rounds of National Family Health Surveys (NFHS) conducted in 1992–1993, 1998–1999 and 2005–2006. The paper analyses women who have completed their reproductive life to understand the characteristics of single-child families. The results show that the proportion of single-child families is higher among the urban, educated and professionally employed women. Among the determinants, age at marriage and first birth, place of residence, economic and education status were significantly associated with single-child families.KEYWORDS: single-child familieslow fertilityNational Family Health SurveyIndia Notes1. An index of couple's education level is computed by combining the educational levels of both husband and wife. The educational level of husband and wife were arranged into four categories—no education, primary school (1–4 years), secondary (5–9 years) and high levels (10 years and above) that were coded as 0 to 3. After combining the educational level of both husband and wife, three categories were made. The combined code of 0 to 2 (low level of education), 3 to 4 (medium level of education) and 5–6 (high level of education).2. In India, infertility rate has decreased from 2 per cent in 1998–1999 to 1.85 per cent in 2005–2006. Childlessness rate has also decreased from 3.01 per cent to 2.65 per cent during the same period (Ganguly & Unisa, Citation2010).3. Marriage is a universal phenomenon in India. As per the 2001 Census, the percentage of never-married adults by age 40 was only about 1 per cent among women (and 3 per cent among men) (India, Registrar General, Citation2001). However, the marriage age has undergone a gradual but steady increase: singulate mean age at marriage increased from 16.8 to 20.2 years for women between 1961 and 2001 (India, Registrar General, Citation2001). Nearly half (45.6 per cent) of Indian women marry before reaching the legally prescribed minimum age at marriage of 18 years.4. One of the background characteristics used in NFHS is an index of economic status of households, known as the wealth index. The economic index was constructed using data on household assets and housing characteristics (IIPS and Macro International, Citation2007). For the present analysis, a standardized wealth index has been calculated for each round of NFHS.
